text:
SUMMARY

This is AI generated summarization, which may have errors. For context, always refer to the full article.

TAIWAN SUPPORTER. A member of security asks a supporter holding a banner in reference to Taiwan to leave the stand, during the badminton men's doubles semifinals at the Paris Olympics in France, August 2, 2024.

Ann Wang/Reuters

PARIS, France – Taiwan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs has condemned the tearing up of a spectator’s banner reading “Taiwan go for it” by another spectator at the badminton venue at theParis Olympicsduring competition on Friday, August 2.

The Taiwanese spectator who was holding the sign posted a video on Facebook showing a man taking her green sign, tearing it in half, and the man being dragged out of the arena by security.

Two Reuters journalists saw the spectator being bodily removed up a staircase, shouting, while Taiwanese shuttler Chou Tien Chen was playing.

Taiwan competes at the Olympics as Chinese Taipei to avoid objections from China, which claims the democratically governed island as its own territory. Taiwan’s government rejects Chinese sovereignty claims.

Green, the color of Taiwan’s ruling Democratic Progressive Party, is used by people who support Taiwan’s separate identity from China and, often, Taiwan independence.

In a statement on Saturday, August 3, Taiwan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs condemned “the crude and despicable means by malicious people to arbitrarily snatch the slogan ‘Taiwan go for it’ from our country’s national during the Olympic Games.

“This violent act is not only ill-educated but also seriously violates the spirit of civilization represented by the Olympic Games, and it is even more contrary to the rule of law and infringes on freedom of expression.”

The island’s de facto ambassador to France, Francois Wu, had reported the incident to local police, the statement said.

Wu posted a picture of himself with the spectator whose banner was destroyed on Facebook, saying: “Thank you to the students who have worked hard to cheer for our badminton heroes! Come on Taiwan!”

The foreign ministry statement said other fans holding banners with the word “Taiwan” on them had been removed from the venue by security.

When asked about the ejections on Saturday, International Olympics Committee spokesman Mark Adams told a news conference that the conditions of entry to Olympic venues were made clear on each ticket.

The terms and conditions of entry to Olympic venues allows the display only of flags of countries and territories participating in the games and forbids any banners that display political messages.

Badminton is a hugely popular sport in both Taiwan and China and both sets of fans have been out in full force at this Olympics.

While some Taiwanese fans have stuck to holding up the official Chinese Taipei Olympic flag, many in the crowd have roared “Taiwan!” when players like Tai Tzu Ying have taken to the court.– Rappler.com

title: Taiwan condemns tearing up of banner at Olympic badminton venue
author: Acor Arceo
description: Badminton is a hugely popular sport in both Taiwan and China and both sets of fans have been out in full force at the Paris Olympics
